hook: You already know how to calculate averages and read a bar graph — but have you ever wondered how a poll can predict an election from just a few thousand responses, or how scientists decide if a new medicine actually works? Raw numbers alone don't tell the whole story; you need tools to collect data carefully, summarize it honestly, and draw conclusions you can trust. In this unit, you'll discover how statisticians turn uncertainty into confident, evidence-based decisions.
concept: The unifying idea is that data is only as useful as the methods used to collect, display, and analyze it — every topic builds toward the formal practice of statistical inference, drawing reliable conclusions about a population from a sample.

topic_roadmap.description: You will calculate and interpret mean, median, and measures of spread like standard deviation to summarize what a data set tells you at a glance.
Master reading and interpreting histograms, box plots, and other displays to identify shape, outliers, and trends in real data sets.
You will evaluate different sampling strategies and recognize how bias can quietly skew results, making a study misleading rather than informative.
Distinguish between surveys, controlled experiments, and observational studies, and explain why only a well-designed experiment can establish cause and effect.
Apply the logic of hypothesis testing to decide whether an observed result is statistically significant or likely just due to chance.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Margin of Error and Confidence Intervals (H)
topic_roadmap.description: You will construct and interpret confidence intervals, understanding how margin of error quantifies the uncertainty in estimates like $\hat{p} \pm E$.
